news_summary: Innovative social initiatives like speed friending at cafes are helping to combat America's friendship recession by fostering new connections and community engagement.
news_summary_fr: Des initiatives sociales innovantes, telles que les rencontres rapides dans les cafés, contribuent à lutter contre la « récession de l'amitié » qui touche les États-Unis en favorisant la création de nouveaux liens et l'engagement communautaire.
news_summary_es: Iniciativas sociales innovadoras, como las sesiones de «speed friending» en cafeterías, están contribuyendo a combatir la «recesión de la amistad» en Estados Unidos al fomentar nuevas relaciones y la participación en la comunidad.

articles.title: Speed friending: one cafe’s answer to America’s growing friendship recession
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY’S EDITORIAL TEAM Something has shifted over the past three decades in how Americans relate to each other. In 1990, about three percent of Americans said they had no close friends. Today, that number sits somewhere between 12 and 20 percent, depending on who you ask. Jaimie Krems, an associate professor of […]
The post Speed friending: one cafe’s answer to America’s growing friendship recession first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY’S EDITORIAL TEAM

Something has shifted over the past three decades in how Americans relate to each other. In 1990, about three percent of Americans said they had no close friends. Today, that number sits somewhere between 12 and 20 percent, depending on who you ask. Jaimie Krems, an associate professor of psychology at UCLA and co-founder of the UCLA Center for Friendship Research, has a name for it: a friendship recession.

“It is painful. It is horrific. The No. 1 antidote to loneliness is friendship,” Krems said. “So we absolutely need to know how friendship works, how people make friends and keep friends.”

Saul Martinez is doing his part to find out. The owner of Cafe Con Arte in downtown Pasco, Washington, started hosting speed-friending nights after his niece moved to town and struggled to meet people. The format borrows from speed-dating: strangers rotate through short conversations at two-person tables, warmed up by ice-breaker questions. What’s a trivial hill you’re willing to die on? Where are you from?

Loneliness has become a structural problem

The friendship recession is not purely a social phenomenon. It tracks alongside some of the biggest structural changes in daily life. Remote work eliminated the casual proximity of office hallways, where friendships used to form almost by accident. Now you can order groceries, work, and scroll through hundreds of faces online without ever speaking to anyone. Social media fills in the gaps with something that looks like a connection but mostly isn’t.

“Honestly, it’s easy to isolate, even unintentionally,” said Ava Robertshaw, who attended the Pasco event. “You can get your groceries delivered, and you can work from home, and you can network and be on social media to feel this sense of connection. But I don’t feel like it actually meets the human need for connection.”

The health stakes are real. Studies show that people with strong friendships live longer and report higher life satisfaction than those who are socially isolated. The flip side is equally stark: chronic loneliness carries roughly the same health risk as smoking up to 15 cigarettes a day.

“Friendship is the silver bullet,” Krems said. “In terms of importance, very few phenomena can do all that friendship can do for us.”

What actually builds lasting adult friendships

Speed-friending events are multiplying across the country as one response to this gap, but Krems is careful about what they can realistically deliver. “I would not be hugely surprised if speed-friending doesn’t always work,” she said. “What I really think does work are low-stakes, repeated interactions with people you’re similar to.”

The essentials, she says, are time and consistent contact, but vulnerability is what tips an acquaintance into an actual friend. She also points to the Benjamin Franklin Effect, a principle from psychology which holds that doing a favor for someone tends to make you view them more warmly. In other words, the exchange of small help-giving is part of how bonds form, not just a byproduct of them.

“There is something about time. There is something about contact. There is something about vulnerability,” Krems said. “But we also need that piece of helping with favors and gift giving.”

The two-hour event might not be the point

Even if a single speed-friending night rarely produces a best friend, participants tend to leave with something. Daniel Madrigal made an actual friend at an earlier Cafe Con Arte event. “We just started talking, and I thought it was a normal conversation. Just normal. And then afterward they were like, ‘Hey, we should totally hang out again,’” he said. They made plans to meet up soon after.

“Finding one good friend or acquaintance here is really the hope, right? You don’t expect to walk away with 17 new friends,” Martinez said.

For Shintell Izquierdo, who attended on a recent Thursday night, the value was harder to quantify. “It was very good for my soul and to be able to meet new people and be able to have conversations I don’t think I would’ve had otherwise if I wouldn’t have come out,” she said.

Robertshaw put it plainly: “You’re socializing with other adults for the sake of socializing. There’s no destination besides just connection. It filled my social bucket.”

Speed-friending may be more spark than lasting flame, but in a country where roughly one in five adults now has no close friends, even a spark has value. “The magic of friendship is something that we should prioritize,” Krems said. These events are not promising lifelong bonds. They are offering something simpler: a room full of strangers who all showed up, which turns out to be its own kind of beginning.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a meaningful community-driven response to a significant social issue—America's growing friendship recession and loneliness epidemic. It describes a tangible, replicable solution (speed-friending events) that fosters human connection and addresses loneliness, which has broad public health and social implications. The story is focused, detailed, and presents an inspiring grassroots initiative with measurable positive impact on people's well-being.
articles.personality_title: A cafe’s speed-friending events offer a new way to tackle loneliness
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Over the past 30 years, more Americans have reported having no close friends. In 1990, about 3% said they had no close friends, but today that number is between 12% and 20%. Experts call this trend a "friendship recession," highlighting a growing loneliness problem.

**What happened** – Saul Martinez, owner of Cafe Con Arte in Pasco, Washington, started hosting speed-friending nights. These events work like speed-dating but for making friends. Strangers have short conversations using ice-breaker questions to get to know each other. The goal is to help people meet others in a friendly, low-pressure setting.

**Impact** – Loneliness is linked to serious health risks, similar to smoking many cigarettes a day. Speed-friending events don’t promise instant lifelong friends, but they create chances for new connections. Attendees say the events help them feel less isolated and give them hope for making new friends. Experts say real friendships need time, repeated contact, and small acts of kindness to grow, which these events can start.

**What's next step** – As more people feel lonely, speed-friending events are growing in popularity across the country. The next step is to keep creating spaces where people meet regularly and can build trust over time. This might lead to stronger community ties and better health for many.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Speed-friending events at local cafes offer a simple, friendly way to help people start new friendships and combat loneliness.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des événements de speed-friending en café pour lutter contre la solitude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis 30 ans, de plus en plus d’Américains déclarent ne pas avoir d’amis proches. En 1990, environ 3 % disaient ne pas en avoir, aujourd’hui ce chiffre est entre 12 % et 20 %. Les experts appellent cela une « récession de l’amitié », soulignant un problème croissant de solitude.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Saul Martinez, propriétaire du Café Con Arte à Pasco, Washington, a commencé à organiser des soirées de speed-friending. Ces événements fonctionnent comme des speed-datings, mais pour se faire des amis. Des inconnus ont de courtes conversations avec des questions pour briser la glace. L’objectif est d’aider les gens à se rencontrer dans un cadre amical et sans pression.

**Impact** – La solitude est liée à de graves risques pour la santé, comparables à ceux du tabac. Les événements de speed-friending ne promettent pas des amitiés instantanées, mais ils créent des opportunités de nouvelles connexions. Les participants disent que ces événements les aident à se sentir moins isolés et à espérer de nouvelles amitiés. Les experts expliquent que de vraies amitiés demandent du temps, des contacts répétés et de petits gestes de gentillesse, ce que ces événements peuvent initier.

**Prochaine étape** – Face à la solitude croissante, les événements de speed-friending se multiplient aux États-Unis. L’étape suivante est de continuer à créer des lieux où les gens peuvent se rencontrer régulièrement et construire la confiance. Cela pourrait renforcer les liens communautaires et améliorer la santé de beaucoup.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les événements de speed-friending dans des cafés locaux offrent une façon simple et conviviale d’aider les gens à commencer de nouvelles amitiés et à lutter contre la solitude.
articles.personality_title_es: Eventos de speed-friending en un café para combatir la soledad
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En los últimos 30 años, más estadounidenses han dicho que no tienen amigos cercanos. En 1990, alrededor del 3 % decía no tenerlos, hoy ese número está entre el 12 % y el 20 %. Los expertos llaman a esto una "recesión de la amistad", señalando un problema creciente de soledad.

**Qué pasó** – Saul Martinez, dueño del Café Con Arte en Pasco, Washington, comenzó a organizar noches de speed-friending. Estos eventos funcionan como citas rápidas, pero para hacer amigos. Personas desconocidas tienen conversaciones breves con preguntas para romper el hielo. El objetivo es ayudar a la gente a conocerse en un ambiente amigable y sin presión.

**Impacto** – La soledad está relacionada con graves riesgos para la salud, comparables a fumar muchos cigarrillos al día. Los eventos de speed-friending no prometen amigos para toda la vida de inmediato, pero crean oportunidades para nuevas conexiones. Los asistentes dicen que estos eventos les ayudan a sentirse menos aislados y a tener esperanza para hacer nuevos amigos. Los expertos dicen que las amistades reales necesitan tiempo, contacto repetido y pequeños actos de ayuda para crecer, algo que estos eventos pueden comenzar.

**Próximo paso** – A medida que más personas se sienten solas, los eventos de speed-friending están creciendo en popularidad en todo el país. El siguiente paso es seguir creando espacios donde la gente pueda reunirse regularmente y construir confianza. Esto podría llevar a comunidades más fuertes y mejor salud para muchos.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los eventos de speed-friending en cafés locales ofrecen una manera sencilla y amigable para que las personas comiencen nuevas amistades y combatan la soledad.
articles.image_prompt: A cozy cafe interior with several small round tables, each set with two empty chairs facing each other, connected by gently glowing, intertwined threads symbolizing new friendships forming; warm, muted earth tones and soft lighting create an inviting atmosphere of connection and hope.
